briefings.headline: Former nun recounts 12 years inside a Carmelite monastery in new memoir
briefings.body: Catherine Coldstream entered a Carmelite monastery at age 24 and spent 12 years living a cloistered life of prayer and obedience before leaving. She has written about her experience in a memoir titled Cloistered: My Years as a Nun, offering a rare firsthand account of life inside a closed religious community.

sources.title: A former nun explains why she ran away from her 'Cloistered' life

briefings.why_it_matters: First-person accounts of cloistered religious life are uncommon, and Coldstream's memoir could offer readers an unusually candid look at the psychological and spiritual realities of monastic commitment.

briefings.headline: West Virginia's paid relocation program is turning temporary remote workers into long-term residents
briefings.body: Ascend West Virginia, launched during the COVID pandemic, offered cash incentives to attract remote workers to the state seeking an economic boost. The program is one of dozens of similar relocation initiatives operating across the country. Early results suggest the strategy is working, with a notable share of participants choosing to stay beyond their initial commitment, signaling that financial incentives could reshape population trends in struggling regions.

sources.title: West Virginia paid digital nomads to move there during COVID. Many are staying

briefings.why_it_matters: For remote workers weighing a move, these programs could mean thousands in relocation cash while potentially revitalizing communities that offer lower costs of living.

briefings.headline: Justice Department drops New York Times subpoenas after judicial pushback
briefings.body: The Justice Department has withdrawn subpoenas seeking grand jury testimony and phone records from New York Times journalists. The move came after a federal judge sharply questioned the government's position during a Thursday hearing. The department's retreat marks a significant reversal in what had been a closely watched confrontation between federal prosecutors and a major news organization over reporter records.

sources.title: Justice Dept. drops 'New York Times' subpoenas under pressure from judge

briefings.why_it_matters: When prosecutors seek journalists' phone records and testimony, it could expose confidential sources and deter whistleblowers from speaking to the press, affecting the public's access to government accountability reporting.

briefings.headline: Best friends discover they are biological siblings after years of close friendship
briefings.body: Andrea Urmston had grown up knowing she had a brother who had been adopted by another family. After years of close friendship with someone she considered her best friend, she discovered that person was her biological brother. The pair had unknowingly built a deep bond before learning the truth of their shared origins.

sources.title: They became best friends - then discovered they were brother and sister

briefings.why_it_matters: The case highlights how adoption records and limited family history could leave people unaware of biological relatives already present in their lives.

briefings.headline: Schools Face a New Literacy Frontier: Teaching Students to Write
briefings.body: As schools have made progress on reading instruction, writing has emerged as the next major challenge in early education. At Sand Creek Elementary in Highlands Ranch, Colorado, first grade teachers are working to build foundational writing skills in young students, guiding them through structured composition exercises. Educators report seeing measurable growth when explicit writing instruction is prioritized, suggesting that focused classroom approaches could make a meaningful difference in student outcomes.

sources.title: The next literacy challenge: Teaching students how to write

briefings.why_it_matters: Students who do not develop strong writing skills early could face lasting disadvantages in academic performance, standardized testing, and eventually workplace communication.

briefings.headline: Rayner Rules Out Rent Controls in England
briefings.body: Housing Secretary Angela Rayner has told the BBC that rent controls will not be introduced in England, arguing that the Renters Rights Act is already having a significant impact on the housing market. Rayner's comments signal the government's position that existing legislation is sufficient to address affordability concerns, closing the door on a policy some tenant advocates have pushed for as rents remain elevated across much of England.

sources.title: Angela Rayner rules out rent controls in England

briefings.why_it_matters: Renters hoping for government-imposed caps on rental costs could now need to look to the Renters Rights Act alone for relief, meaning landlords retain the ability to set market-rate rents across England.

quick_hits.text: Brent crude surged over 6% to hit $100 per barrel Thursday for the first time since May, driven by escalating Middle East conflict.

source.title: Oil prices hit $100 for the first time since May

quick_hits.text: Google has been fined $1 billion by the EU under the Digital Markets Act, becoming the third tech giant penalized under the landmark law.

source.title: Google hit with $1 billion in fines as EU braces for Trump battle

quick_hits.text: The Justice Department withdrew subpoenas targeting New York Times reporters, with lead newsroom lawyer David McCraw confirming the reversal to NPR.

source.title: New York Times lawyer talks about DOJ withdrawing subpoenas sent to Times' reporters

quick_hits.text: A historic cyclospora outbreak is devastating Michigan residents, with some sufferers reporting up to 20 bathroom trips daily amid a multistate spread.

source.title: Using a bathroom 20 times a day - life in the middle of US cyclospora outbreak

quick_hits.text: John Pardon won the Fields Medal for groundbreaking work bridging geometry, topology, and physics.

source.title: John Pardon Untangles the Theoretical Loops

voice_blocks.title: This Day
voice_blocks.text: 2025: Angara Airlines Flight 2311 crashes on approach to Tynda Airport, killing all 48 people on board.

closer.text: "The press was protected so that it could bare the secrets of government and inform the people."
